summary: I was born in San Diego, California, which is where my life began before I moved elsewhere. Although I didn't spend much of my adult life there, San Diego is significant as my birthplace and the starting point of my journey. It’s a place that marks the beginning of my story.

In Oklahoma, my mixed African American and American Indian heritage wasn’t always recognized, so I had to live life primarily identifying as African American. That experience shaped a lot of how I see racial identity and community. It was a place where I learned about the complexities of race and ethnicity.

I regularly participate in what some call the Pentecostal faith, though I consider myself non-denominational. Pentecostalism is part of my religious experience, but I also incorporate elements from other traditions. It’s a faith that connects me spiritually and culturally in my life.

Catholicism is part of my family’s religious background, which I respect and incorporate into my own heritage. Though I chose my own spiritual path, the Catholic Church’s influence is a thread in my family history. It represents one of the many religious traditions that shaped my upbringing.

Protestantism is another strong part of my family’s religious background, alongside Catholicism and the Church of God. While I am non-denominational, I acknowledge the Protestant roots in my heritage. It’s part of the religious tapestry that informs who I am today.

The Church of God, based in Anderson, Indiana, is part of my family’s religious history. It’s one of the denominations that influenced my heritage, along with Catholicism and Protestantism. Though I chose a non-denominational path, this church’s background is part of my spiritual lineage.

I have been deeply involved in the labor movement for over twenty years, and it’s a core part of my identity. The labor movement connects me to community activism and social justice, and I participate actively on a daily basis. It’s been a powerful force in my life, shaping my work and my values.

Local 3488, based at Harborview Medical Center in Seattle, is my union home where I’ve held many leadership roles. I am currently Vice President-Acting President, and I’ve served as secretary, treasurer, and on the executive board. This local union has been central to my labor activism and professional life.

I’ve worked at Harborview Medical Center’s Emergency Room for nearly twenty-two years as a Patient Service Specialist. Harborview is the only level one trauma center for seven states, and it’s where I coordinate critical care and support for patients. It’s a demanding but rewarding workplace where I’m a constant resource for many people.

Seattle is where I live and work, particularly at Harborview Medical Center. It’s a city that has shaped much of my professional and union life. I’m deeply connected to the community here, both through my job and my union involvement.

Washington state is where I live, work, and engage in union leadership and community activism. I’ve held statewide roles in the Washington Federation of State County Municipal Employees and represent various organizations here. It’s the region where my labor and social justice work is most active.

Puyallup, Washington, is one of my primary places of residence. I live there while also maintaining ties to Seattle and Tacoma. It’s part of the Puget Sound area where I balance my personal and professional life.

For about the past year, I’ve also had a residence in Tacoma, Washington, expanding my connection to the greater Puget Sound region. Tacoma is part of the area I live and work around, alongside Seattle and Puyallup. It’s an important part of my geographic and community life.

entity: Washington Federation of State County Municipal Employees
summary: I am actively involved in the Washington Federation of State County Municipal Employees, where I currently serve as Vice President-Acting President. I also sit on the statewide Executive Committee, representing members across the state. This organization is a key part of my union leadership and advocacy work in Washington.

entity: American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees
summary: I represent the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME) in various capacities, including at the state and local levels. AFSCME is a major union umbrella that supports workers like me in public service. Being part of AFSCME connects me to a larger labor movement beyond just my local union.

I am proud to represent the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists (CBTU) both regionally and nationally. I also serve on the national women's committee for CBTU, which focuses on empowering Black union members. This organization is important to me as it connects my labor activism with my identity and community.

Early in my career, I was a member of the Service Employees International Union (SEIU) both at Swedish Medical Center and the University of Washington. I stayed with SEIU for about four to five years before moving to another union. SEIU was my introduction to union membership and activism.

Before working at Harborview, I was employed at Swedish Medical Center where I was a member of SEIU. This was part of my early healthcare career and union involvement. Swedish Medical Center helped shape my understanding of healthcare work and labor organizing.

I worked at the University of Washington early on, where I was a member of SEIU and began my union activism. It was there that I met important mentors like Althea Lew and John Frazier who helped pull me into the labor movement. The University was a foundational place for my growth as a union leader.

Althea Lew was a close friend of Ms. Juanita and Berlin Chicana and a strong woman in the labor movement who helped bring me into union activism. She was part of the early group of African American activists at the University of Washington when I started. Althea’s example and support were instrumental in my journey.

John Frazier was one of the founding African American activists at the University of Washington who influenced me from the very beginning of my union involvement. He was part of the strong group of leaders who shaped the labor movement in the eighties and beyond. John’s presence helped guide my early steps in activism.

entity: American Federation of Labor and Congress of Industrial Organizations
summary: The American Federation of Labor and Congress of Industrial Organizations (AFL-CIO) is a major umbrella organization for labor unions that I have engaged with through my union work. I attended my first AFL-CIO meeting in Cleveland, where I was inspired by the national president who encouraged me to keep going. The AFL-CIO’s history, including its ties to civil rights and labor rights movements, deeply resonates with me.

Martin Luther King Jr. is a historic figure whose work intersected with the labor movement, particularly in the sixties when he marched for labor rights. His leadership helped highlight the importance of fair wages and working conditions, which is foundational to the unions I am part of today. I respect his legacy as part of the broader struggle for justice that includes labor rights.

April Sims is the first African American to hold a head position in the AFL-CIO for the state of Washington, which is a historic and inspiring achievement. I am proud to be part of the labor movement in Washington during her leadership. April’s role represents progress and empowerment for Black union members like myself.
cues: first African American head in Washington AFL-CIO

summary: At Harborview Medical Center, I often greet medics and American Medical Response (AMR) personnel as they bring patients into the emergency room. I coordinate care with AMR and other emergency services to ensure patients receive timely treatment. AMR is a critical partner in the trauma care network I work within daily.
